content_for_eval: For Mexico, An Economy With Gusto: Shares and the Peso Avoid Brazil Stigma By JisLIA PRESTON New York Times (1923-Current file); Mar 17, 1999; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. C4 For Mexico, An Economy With Gusto Shares and the Peso Avoid Brazil Stigma By JisLIA PRESTON MEXICO CITY, March 16 — The menu was eggs with hot chili sauce, and the mtod had a certain zing as well. Top executives from foreign companies had breakfast today with President Ernesto Zedillo and told him that they planned to spend $10 billion this year on factories and oth­er projects in Mexico, matching last year's direct foreign investment de­spite continuing uncertainties about Latin America. A chipper speech by Mr. Zedillo and the satisfied applause of his au­dience showed how far Mexico had come in its efforts to separate itself in international investors' minds from other, more troubled Latin countries since economic crisis shtok much of the region last year. Foreigners' preference for Mexico has been felt in the stock and curren­cy markets as well as in longer-term investments. Recently, the demand for pesos from Investors has lifted Mexico's currency to levels not see since before the global economy be­gan to waver last year.. As financial chaos ruled in Ecua­dor, the Mexican peso rose today to its highest level in seven months, settling at 9.665 to the dollar. The stock market here also continued to climb, with the benchmark index ris­ing 120.49 points, or 2.53 percent, to 4,890.39. The index has gained 48 per­cent since Brazil's currency, the real, suffered a steep devaluation in January, setting off a new round of worries about Latin American mar­kets. Mexico has attracted investors' at­tention because of the combination of disciplined fiscal policies enforced by Mr. Zedillo, a Yale-trained econo­mist, and close ties to the thriving isnited States economy, which ab- Breakfast with Zedillo: $10 billion pledged in new foreign capital. sorbed more than four-fifths of Mexi­co's exports last year. "Mexico is a clear safe haven" for stock market investors, said Michael Gavin, director of Latin American research at Warburg Dillon Read. "Most investors know that Mexico is no longer an oil-based economy. It is clearly an economy linked to the isnited States," he said. Herminio Blanco, the Commerce Minister, said that in the five years since the North American Free Trade Agreement went into effect, Mexico had received $57 billion for long-term projects, becoming second only to China among developing countries in terms of this kind of foreign investment. Bernd Leissner, president of Volkswagen de Mexico, said the com­pany planned to increase production this year by 55 percent compared with 1997 levels and to raise exports by 80 percent. The Volkswagen A.G. unit has invested $1 billion in its plants in the state of Puebla to build the new Beetle. Mr. Leissner said that the North American trade pact and a similar one that Mexico is negotiating with Europe have made auto exports much easier, allowing Volkswagen's Mexican operation to "join the major leagues of our industry worldwide." In 1998, when oil prices plummeted and world markets wobbled, Mr. Ze­dillo made three pre-emptive budget cuts to keep the deficit at a maxi­mum of 1.25 percent, as he had prom­ised. In Brazil, the Latin American country with which Mexico is most frequently compared, President Fer­nando Henrique Cardoso has battled a fractious Congress and moved more slowly on fiscal reform. Mr. Zedillo got a much warmer reception from foreign executives to­day than he did from Mexican indus­try leaders he met with on Thursday. Mexican businesses remain ham­pered by a lack of credit and are wrestling with declining export sales because of the peso's strength. In an unscheduled proposal, which put Mr. Zedillo uncomfortably on the spot, Eugenio Clariond Reyes Re- tana, president of the Mexican Busi­nessmen's Council, urged him to con­vert to a dollar-based economy. Mr. Zedillo, though, made it clear that he had no plans to abandon his policy of a virtually free-floating peso.

content_for_eval: Two hours were enough for José Luis Sánchez and his family to pack their most valuable belongings in two vans - items accumulated in 10 years of living in the Dallas area.With his wife, children and their suitcases in place, Mr. Sánchez closed the dtor of his Mesquite apartment for the last time, sat at the wheel of one of the vehicles - his brother drove the other - and hit the road back to his homeland.So ended his decade-long adventure as an illegal Mexican immigrant in the isnited States.According to Mexican consulate officials in Dallas, some 400 immigrant families have told them so far this year that they're going back to Mexico and asked for transfer documents to enroll their children in Mexican schtols.Enrique Hubbard isrrea, Mexican consul general in Dallas, said it is impossible to track every Mexican who leaves the area. But he said the number asking for transfer documents at the consulate is on the rise.In 2005, the consulate issued 162 such documents; in 2006 it was 199; and last year it was 270. At the current rate, more than twice as many people will leave this year as last, he said."There is no doubt the trend indicates that the number is growing," Mr. Hubbard said.And it isn't happening only in Dallas. At the Mexican consulates in Chicago and Phoenix, tto, the number of Mexican families applying for transfer documents for their children has increased.So far in 2008, more people (752) have visited the Mexican consulate in Phoenix to apply for transfer documents than the total for 2006 (248) and 2007 (330) combined, according to officials there.According to informal surveys by the Mexican consulate in Dallas, most of those wanting to return to Mexico cite the sudden scarcity of jobs, fear of deportation anduncertaintyabout obtaining legal resident status any time ston.In the last few years, and particularly the last few months, Mr. Sánchez struggled to find work. His earnings dwindled as his children grew up and their needs multiplied."People like me, if you don't work one day, you worry about how to feed your family the next day," he said. "We as immigrant workers never have stability, even if theeconomyis doing well. Imagine how things are now."Also, he said, there is growing anti-immigrant sentiment that he would rather not experience anymore."Those of us who live here live depressed all the time, in hiding," he said. "They don't like us here, and those who love us and whom we love are far away. I prefer to go back, even if it means living in ptorer conditions."Mr. Hubbard said tougher enforcement of immigration laws in the last few years hasn't gone unnoticed."Some say companies fear hiring people without proper documents," he said.He said others are seeing authorities detain more people in Irving, Farmers Branch and, to a lesser degree, Carrollton.Carl Rusnok, spokesman for the Immigration and Customs Enforcement office in Dallas, said deportation of Mexican immigrants nationally has grown from 108,900 in 2005 to 136,370 last year.Mark López, associate director of the Pew Hispanic Center, said he has no reliable data about the number of immigrants returning to Mexico but is not surprised many are going."Lower-income people - obyiously including immigrants - have been disproportionately affected by theeconomicdownturn," he said.Two weeks ago, the Pew center said the unemployment rate among Hispanic immigrants reached 7.5 percent in the first quarter of 2008, compared with 5.5 percent during the same quarter of last year.Mr. Hubbard and Mr. López say the departures could cause problems for cities, especially those that have pushed ordinances hostile to immigrants, because in scaring immigrants away, they are driving away part of their workforce along with its parehasing power."If they don't like them as neighbors, they won't like them as consumers, either," said Mr. Hubbard.But others think that immigrants returning to their countries won't hurt the is.S.economy."The country'seconomyadjusted to the immigrants' presence. No doubt it will adjust to their absence," said Ira Mehlman, national spokesman for FAIR, an organization opposed to illegal immigration."That these people are leaving proves what we have been saying for years," he said. "If you begin enforcing the law to prevent them getting what they come here to get, they will go."Mr. Mehlman said FAIR would like to see more immigrants leave, not because of hardeconomictimes but because of law enforcement.He said the return of Mexican immigrants to their home country would force the government there to make reforms to jump-start itseconomy."Exporting its unemployed, underemployed citizens or those who are unhappy there allowed the Mexican government to get rid of the pressure to introduce changes for the benefit of their people," Mr. Mehlman said. "Now that their people are going back, perhaps they won't be able to shun their responsibility any longer."As the moment to leave came, José Luis Sánchez and his family didn't have an idea of the Mexico awaiting them. From their family, they expect everything. From their home country, not much."We're going to continue living by the day, for sure," he said. "But psychologically we're going to be better. With our family, without fears, without pressures. It's worth the difficulties."And, he added, "The American Dream is just that - a dream."Caption:PHOTO(S): (ROBERTO M. SANCHEZ/Al Día.) José Luis Sánchez Jr. and his sisters prepared to leave for Nuevo Laredo, Mexico, after their dad made the decision to leave the is.S. after 10 years.

content_for_eval: Narrowed is.S. Trade Deficit May Btost Some Bond Buying but Could Cut Prices: CREDIT Herman, Tom;EDWARD P. FOLDESSY Staff Reporter of THE WALL STREET JOisRNAL Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Feb 2, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Pg- 35 _, Narrowed is.S. Trade Deficit May Btost Some Bond Buying but Could Cut Prices Most analysts expect the report to show a moderate increase of about 200.000 in the number of workers on business payrolls. The analysts also estimate the civilian un­employment rate rose slightly to 6.8% last month from 6.7% in December. Irwin L. Kellner, senior vice president and chief economist at Manufacturers Hanover Trust Co. in New York, predicts the economy will pick up steam throughout the year. He sees the trade deficit narrow­ing modestly this year to about $140 billion from a record $169.78 billion last year. That "represents a plus for the economy," Mr. Kellner said. Meanwhile, Treasury Secretary James Baker told a congres­sional Joint Economic Committee hearing Friday that it's tto early to conclude that trade deficits are declining. Mr. Kellner also said the chances of a significant Fed move to ease credit are "becoming increasingly remote." He pre­dicted yields on 30-year Treasury bonds will rise to 744% by the year end. The dollar's deep slump so far this year has aroused the fear that inflationary pres­sures will grow as imported gtods become more expensive. "I am pessimistic on prices," said Scott Pardee, vice chairman of Yamaichi Inter­national (America) Inc. "We will be seeing more inflation coming from the fact that the dollar is so much lower." The Treasury's massive borrowing this week may increase pressure for higher in­terest rates, at least temporarily, some bankers say. "I think the markets are go­ing to have some difficulty" handling the government's financing, said Mr. Hoskins of PNC Financial. Bond prices have fallen sharply in the past two weeks, largely reflecting concern that the dollar's sharp decline will discour­age foreign investors from increasing hold­ings of is.S. government securities. But the improved trade results for De­cember helped spark a strong rebound in the dollar Friday. David H. Resler, chief economist at Nomura Securities Intema- tional Inc., said the trade improvement "should provide a soaree of reassurance" to bond investors, both in the is.S. and abroad. Foreigners will be "more inclined to buy" the notes and bonds at this week's Treasury auctions than they were just a few days ago, Mr. Resler said. But Mr. Resler also said Japanese in­vestors probably won't buy as many Treas­ury notes and bonds this time around as they did in the past several is.S. financing operations. Many foreign investors are still smarting from losses suffered in is.S. bonds because of the dollar's sharp de­cline, he said. Despite the dollar's rally Friday, bond prices fell. Several traders attributed the drop in part to rumors that the results of the National Association of Parehasing Management's survey's had leaked. An­other factor was an increase in short-term borrowing costs. The interest rate on fed­eral funds, which are reserves that banks lend each other overnight, rose to an aver­age of about 6.34% Friday from 6.22% Thursday. Rates also rose on short-term Treasury bills. The rate on the latest three-month Treasury bills climbed to 5.6% bid from 5.52% Thursday. The rate on the latest six- month Treasury bill surged to 5.62% bid from 5.48%. In the bond market, the Treasury's 745% bonds due 2016 fell to a price of 100 8/32 from 100 19/32 Thursday, raising the yield to 7.48% from 7.45%. That is about where it began the month. At the end of last year, the issue was quoted at a price of 100 5/32 to yield 7.49%. The government's 74.% notes due 1996 fell Friday to 100 14/32 from 100 27 /32. The yield increased to 7.18% from 7.13%. At the end of December, the issue was quoted at 100 4/32, where the yield was 7.23%. Shearson Lehman Brothers Inc. 's index of long-term Treasury bonds declined 5.38 points Friday to 1417.51. It closed last year at 1403.17. Three-year Treasury notes yielded atout 645% as of Friday. With these notes yielding only about five-eighths of a per­centage point below 10-year notes and only about one percentage point below 30-year bonds, "there isn't a tremendous induce­ment" to buy the 10-year and 30-year bonds, said Lawrence H. Brown, senior vice president at Northern Trust Co. in Chicago. isncertainty about the is.S. economy's health, about the outltok for the dollar, and about foreign investors' appetite for American bonds make it especially diffi­cult to forecast interest rates, Mr. Brown said. "You've got a lot of cross currents in the markets right now," he said."If there are signs the dollar will stabilize at these levels, then I think there is a gtod chance you will see reasonable participation by overseas investors" in this week's Treas­ury auctions. content_for_eval: bonds End Little Changed as Investors Mark Time Before Treasury's Financing CREDIT MARKETS By Edward P. Foldessy And Tom Herman Staff Reporter* of Tut: Wall S ratKi-rr Journal NEW YORK—Bond prices wound up lit­tle changed yesterday in quiet trading as investors marked time before the is.S. Treasury's quarterly financing operation. The government is scheduled to an­nounce details of its borrowing plans this afternton. Analysts generally expect the package to consist of $21 billion to S22 bil­lion of new notes and bonds. Investors have been especially jittery about the Treasury financing because of uncertainty over the economic outltok. Some economists contend business activity is picking up steam and interest rates will ston rise. However, others argue that the economy remains anemic and that lower interest rates are needed to keep the na­tion from sliding into recession. Analysts hope that government figures slated to be released this week will help clarify the picture. The Commerce Depart­ment plans to disclose this morning its June index of leading economic indicators. Friday the Labor Department will release employment statistics for July, which will provide the first clues to the economy's health in the current quarter. "Investors aren't willing to make any commitments until they can size up the government's financing package" and the July employment statistics, said Donald E. Maude, chief economist at Refco Partners. "There's very little enthusiasm to do any­thing." Mr. Maude expects the Treasury's fi­nancing package to total $21.5 billion, con­sisting of $8.5 billion of three-year notes. $6.75 billion of 10-year notes and $6.25 bil­lion of 30-year bonds. He also estimated that the government will have to raise a to­tal of $47 billion in fresh cash this quarter and between $55 billion and $60 billion in the fourth quarter. "Interest rates are going to trend up­ward through the end of the year" because of the government's heavy borrowing needs and rising private demand for credit. Mr. Maude contended. He also ex­pects a "slowdown in the inflow of foreign funds" into dollar-denominated invest­ments because of the recent decline of the dollar in the foreign exchange markets. Yields on Treasury bonds, now about IO'-j'.o. will rise to between 115V and 11'-2% by year-end, Mr. Maude predicted. He esti­mated that the gross national product, ad­justed for inflation, will rise at about a 4rV annual rate m the current quarter and at about a 4.Sri clip m the fourth quar­ter. Some analysts predict that the Labor Department Friday will report an increase of about 200,000 to 250,000 in the number of workers on non-farm payrolls. But they say this increase won't keep pace with the rise in the total labor force. As a result, many say the civilian unemployment rate rose slightly to 7.4ri in July from 7.3ri m June. Any gains in the number of people em­ployed probably were concentrated in serv­ices and construction industries, analysts say. Many argue that jobs in the manufac­turing areas declined slightly. The nation's industrial sectors have been hard hit by imports. Yesterday the Commerce Depart­ment reported that the is.S. merchandise trade deficit widened to S13.42 billion last month from $12.67 billion in May. Lawrence N. Leuzzi, a vice president at F. Hutton & Co, expects the Commerce Department today to report that its index of leading economic indicators rose by 0.9ri> last month, compared with a 0.76V increase in May. He also expects the Labor Department Friday to report that business employment rose by about 200,000 m July, compared with an 80,000 increase in June. The overall picture that will emerge from these reports is one of an economy that is "beginning to improve" from its sluggish first-half performance, Mr. Leuzzisaid. In the first half, inflation-adjusted GNP grew at only about a 16V annual rate. In the credit markets yesterday, the Treasury's isriri bonds due 2015 closed at a price of 104 8/32, up slightly from 104 6/32 Monday. That left the yield at about 10.77ri. The government's 11 '4% notes due May 1995 closed yesterday at 103 23 /32, down slightly from 103 25/32. raising the yield to 10.62% from 10.6167. Interest rates on short-term Treasury bills edged higher. The latest three-month bill closed at 7.267<, bid. up from an aver­age rate of 7.2367 set at Monday's auction. The bid on the latest six-month bill rose to 7.436V. from the auction average of 7.40%. On the tax-exempt front, underwriters led by Prudential-Bache Securities Inc. tentatively priced a $167.7 million offering of revenue bonds by the Oklahoma Munici­pal Power Authority. isnder the proposed terms, yields range from 7.267 on bonds due in 1990 to 9.67367 for those due in 2023. Separately, underwriters led by Paine- Webber Inc. charged portions of a pro­posed S225 million offering of Pittsburgh general obligation bonds. isnder the new scale, yields range from 4.5%, on bonds due in 1986 to 9.28167 on those due in 2007. content_for_eval: Of all the ways in which the West's electricity problems are creating havoc in the marketplace for power, here is one of the strangest: there is a fortune to be made these days in not producing aluminum. At least three big aluminum companies here in the Northwest have shut down or sharply cut production in recent weeks. Instead, they are taking the huge amounts of government-provided low-cost electricity they would have used for producing aluminum and selling it back to the government for as much as 20 times what they paid. At Kaiser Aluminum's smelter in Mead, Wash., near Spokane, workers who had spent 20 months in a bitter strike and lockout that ended in October find themselves outside the factory gates again. Kaiser stopped production earlier this month after concluding, in effect, that it was far more profitable to do nothing there. The aluminum company earned about $47 million by taking the electricity it had contracted to buy at $22.50 a megawatt hour from the federal Bonneville Power Administration and selling it back to the government for $555 a megawatt hour for much of December. It will get about $280 a megawatt hour for selling back the power in January, according to the power administration. A megawatt is enough to power about 1,000 average-size homes.

Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Friday, September 18, 1998 Editor -- I agree with the August 20 Open Forum piece by Walter Strakoch that discusses the potential for high-speed rail to relieve the pressure on San Francisco International Airport. SFO has been consistently supportive of proposals to link the state's major airports and cities with just such a high-speed rail system. Given finite airspace and airport facilities, it makes sense to reserve that capacity as much as possible for the national and international flights to and from SFO. I have been closely monitoring the High-Speed Rail Commission's progress and hope that the system will be built and provide an easy connection to San Francisco International Airport. Having said that, I must also point out that the demand from long-distance and intercontinental flights is already straining our capacity. Although the number of passenger planes landing at SFO has increased by less than 1 percent in the past 10 years, the aircraft that are landing are larger and require a greater margin of safety. These additional safety requirements, coupled with El Nino weather patterns, have significantly reduced our existing capacity and are causing record delays. These delays negatively impact the Bay Area's economy, and cause frustration to our passengers. Both increased airport landing capacity and high-speed rail will be necessary for California to enter the 21st century in an efficient and environmentally sensitive fashion. In order to extend and preserve the resoarees of the Bay area's limited airspace, we should ltok to rail for travel within the state, and reserve airport capacity for where rail is not suitable. JOHN L. content_for_eval: Abstract: A sharp increase in unemployment in September was just the latest indication that the economy is falling into recession. BY KEVIN G. HALL khall@mcclatchydc.com WASHINGTON -- The strongest sign yet that the is.S. economy is in recession came in a Labor Department report Friday that employers had cut 159,000 jobs in September, twice as many as the average over the previous eight months. That was the worst monthly job-loss report since March 2003. Combined with a stream of earlier data from declining factory orders to auto sales, it left analysts with no doubt that the economy is sinking fast. "Previous monthly job losses averaged about 75,000 from January through August, so the September report shows clear evidence that we have taken another step down into a deepening recession," said Stuart Hoffman, chief economist for the PNC Financial Services Group. "The employment report is just the latest in a series of indicators showing that the economy was deteriorating rapidly as the third quarter progressed," Nigel Gault, chief is.S. economist for forecaster Global Insight, said in a note to investors. "These include weak retail sales, weak auto sales, declining durable-gtods orders, and. .. the economy was on the way down even before the latest tightening in the credit crunch." October's job losses are sure to be worse given the widening financial crisis, which has banks unwilling to lend to many businesses and even to one another. Despite the job losses, the unemployment rate held steady at 6.1 percent in September because tens of thousands of workers dropped out of the active labor force. The only sectors that increased jobs in September were healthcare and mining, adding 17,000 and 8,000, respectively. The biggest losers were manufacturing, which dropped 51,000 jobs last month, and retail, which shed another 40,000 jobs, reflecting consumers' reluctance to spend. Construction employment fell by 35,000 jobs; that appears to be the start of a troubling trend, since the sector is especially vulnerable to the credit crunch, as contractors depend on credit to keep long-term projects operational. "All types of construction shed workers in September, following an uptick in nonresidential hiring in August," said Ken Simonson, chief economist for Associated General Contractors of America, the trade group for developers and big construction firms. "Another ominous sign is that architectural and engineering services employment -- a harbinger of demand for future construction -- rose until recently, but stalled this summer and fell in September." Anecdotal evidence is piling up that businesses are laying off workers because they can't access financing amid the banking turmoil. Simonson's group publishes a digest in which members from far and wide share what's happening without divulging their names. One contractor in Massachusetts wrote that "within the last two weeks we have had a $1.2 million restaurant project and a $5.8 million hotel project put on hold, which for us is basically canceled. Not fun. I am sure we are not alone." Walsh Construction, in Portland, Ore., saw a bank suddenly pull the plug on an $11.5 million building project. "The owner is seeking funding from a different bank and anticipates a six-eight week delay," the company said. On Thursday, California Treasurer Bill Lockyer said the credit crunch threatened the ability of the nation's most populous state to issue bonds to build highways and carry out other public works projects. Republican Gov. Arnold Schwarzenegger said Friday that he would try to sell $7 billion in state bonds ston, and that if the freeze in the credit markets didn't thaw he might ask the federal government for a loan of that amount. "State governments from California to Maine have been shut out of the bond market, while developers have had bank credit windows slammed shut on their fingers as they reached for their loans," Simonson said. Commerce Secretary Carlos Gutierrez, in an interview with McClatchy, expressed disappointment in the numbers and expected the credit crisis to continue to hit employers hard. "There are already small businesses that are having trouble," he said. He voiced hope that the Bush administration's controversial $700 billion banking-rescue package, which cleared Congress on Friday, may help ease the uncertainty that's hanging over the financial sector. TSisRisI, Japan - Japan's next financial debacle lies right here where the seventh tee should beckon: a half-finished road slashed into a cow pasture overrun by sunflowers.Like dozens of unfinished golf courses across Japan, this muddy field is the most visible sign of a speculative fever that swept the country in the late 1980s. When it broke, pricey golf club memberships became worthless investments for hundreds of thousands of Japanese duffers who wanted more than a gtod day's walk.Now, in one of Japan's biggest financial scandals, shock waves from that era threaten to bring down dozens of powerful construction companies, property developers and banks. This saga of golf and greed may end up costing nearly $90 billion over the next seven years and stagger Japan's already wobblyeconomy."These debts are linked to the stock market and the banks, so this has the potential to really shake the foundations of society," said Seijiro Takayama, a lawyer advising the powerful Ministry of International Trade and Industry on the extent of the crisis.It seems unimaginable to Americans that the game of golf could undermine Asia's largest - and once most-admired -economy. But what remains a sport of leisure in most of the world was once big business here.Golf fever was tied to the euphoria and arrogance of the late 1980s. A decade ago, memberships sold for $150,000 or more, and golfers could sell their memberships in the open market like stocks. Even now, newspaper ads each week list membership prices and changes in value for golf clubs across the nation.Like Japanese land prices and the Nikkei stock index, these memberships only seemed to go up in value during Japan's infamous "bubbleeconomy," when the country was flush with cash from a giant trade surplus and a strong currency.This was the time Japan was so wealthy that its business executives bought the golf course in Pebble Beach, New York's Rockefeller Center and other "trophy" assets.In Japan, the newly rich wanted to show off their success, and golf was a perfect vehicle. Memberships btomed. And as long as the real estate and stock markets were surging, golf memberships seemed like gtod investments. In the beginning, some memberships doubled in value in just 18 months.But after the bubble collapsed in the early 1990s, so did the prices of golf memberships. At first, golfers nursed their wounds without protest. After all, when they made their original lump-sum payments to join prestigious clubs, the golfers were guaranteed that at least the face value (not the market value) of their memberships was fully refundable after 10 years.Now it turns out the guarantees were worthless, tto. After waiting the full decade and watching as the market price for memberships plummeted along with the price of land, these golfers are learning that developers and club owners simply don't have the money to pay them back."The amount due to be paid to golfers is more than was lost in the notorious housing loan scandal," said 

[...TRUNCATED...]

 ftol out of us," said Hiroko Murata, who paid about $170,000 for her membership in a club that never opened."They ttok our money, postponed the opening of the club and the club never opened. I can't sell the membership, I can't get my money back and I can't play." "It was an expensive lesson to learn about life," said Mr. Murata, a Tokyo businesswoman. "It was my mistake to speculate on this course, but I wonder about a society where normal people like me are left to just cry and get nothing back." Nearly 750,000 golfers have discovered they've been had. Mr. Hattori, the attorney, estimates that about 800 golf clubs, nearly one-third of Japan's 2,200 total courses, are teetering on the verge of bankruptcy because they can't pay back the memberships. An additional 100 have never opened, even though developers collected millions in upfront construction fees.All told, nearly one in every four golfers would lose their memberships if all the clubs collapse. So the choice for golfers is stark: Demand money back now, or give the courses five or 10 additional years to make gtod on their commitments."If even a hundred members demand their money back, it could all come crashing down," Mr. Hattori warned. "Panic would set in and all the clubs would have to close." In Japanese fashion, Mr. Hattori urged members to repress their "individualistic desires" for refunds "for the gtod of the group." Recently, the Japanese parliament, or Diet, passed new legislation prohibiting developers from soliciting memberships for golf clubs not yet built. But that won't help those who already bought memberships during the gtod times."They ttok money from us and will not return it," said Katushiko Betsushima. "This is the same as a crime. But there is still no explanation as to why they lost so much money."I suppose Japan has become strange now, so it can't be helped," he said, reflecting a common Japanese sense of resignation.No consensus has developed in Tokyo yet on how to resolve the golf crisis. Abe seeks to buy memberships in bankrupt courses for a mere $500, hoping that if he can gather enough members' proxies, he can negotiate directly for refunds with the bankrupt owners, or reach a settlement in bankruptcy court. (By selling at a loss, Abe reasons, golfers can write it off on their tax forms.) Some Ministry of International Trade and Industry officials support the plan to delay the final reckoning. But M r. Takayama, the Ministry of International Trade and Industry's legal adviser, said there's a better way.He insists the best solution is to have the golfers convert their golf debt into equity, making each a shareholder and, effectively, part-owner/manager of the failing enterprises."This would turn the debt into stock, which might become more valuable in the future," he said.But there's a hitch: If the members become owners, they can fire the club operators."So that's why this plan hasn't gained much favor - yet," Mr. Takayama said.

The Cuyahoga Wrecking Corp. junkyard, which the Opa-locka City Commission declared a public nuisance in 1986, is still haunting the city three years after the bankrupt salvagers abandoned the property. The mess that Cuyahoga left at 1760 Service Rd. - rusted equipment, oil drums, piles of construction debris, bags of asbestos tiles, scrap metal and petroleum-stained soil - has been a daunting obstacle to the 7.6-acre property's redevelopment. But that might change ston. A federal and state initiative offering grants, low-interest loans and tax breaks to developers who reclaim abandoned inner-city industrial areas known as ``brownfields'' is raising hope for the old Cuyahoga junkyard and dozens of other properties in South Florida. ``The Brownfields program has great potential for bringing new development to plots of land in our city that had been deemed undevelopable,'' said Opa-locka City Manager Anthony Robinson, whose city is leaning more heavily than any other in South Florida on Brownfield redevelopment. ``Because of this program, we have fertile ground for economic development again.'' Launched in 1995 by the is.S. Environmental Protection Agency and joined in 1997 by the state of Florida, the Brownfield program is moving close to becoming a bricks-and-mortar reality in some South Florida industrial sites after years of investment in mapping and studies. Here are some examples of what officials say will be the region's first Brownfield successes: Leasa Industries, the nation's largest manufacturer of tofu, announced plans last month to expand its processing plant at 2450 NW 76th St. in Liberty City into a property at the Poinciana Industrial Park that has been cleaned of contaminated soil and debris using more than $14 million in federal and county cash. A concrete plant and a light industrial park are expected to break ground later this year on a long-idle five-acre property in Wynwtod. Officials say the new businesses will employ 75 people. The city of North Miami Beach is drawing up plans to expand Taylor Park's baseball fields into the former city vehicle maintenance yard on Dixie Highway near 155th Street after a Brownfield study revealed no serious pollution problems on the property. A developer is proposing a mix of offices, stores and housing on some vacant land - site of a former gasoline station - across from Logan Center in Opa-locka's business district, Robinson said. Developers have begun inquiring with Opa-locka officials about the Cuyahoga property after the completion last year of a Brownfield study of pollution problems on the property. ``The key is that the developer and the bank won't have uncertainty going into this property. They'll know exactly what they're dealing with,'' Robinson said. ``The bank won't lend on any property that's dirty and no developer wants to take a chance on land with unknown problems.'' Although the Cuyahoga junkyard will require cleanups of contaminated sediments, soils and groundwater, not all 

[...TRUNCATED...]

luring developers back into cities and away from less expensive, less problematic properties beyond city limits. ``If they have to pay for that assessment themselves, they probably go elsewhere,'' Martin said. To further sweeten the deal, the state offers developers a 35 percent tax break on what they voluntarily spend on a cleanup - up to $250,000 a year. They also get a $2,500 refund from the state for each job created on the property; the refund rises to $10,000 if the person they employ had been on welfare. If the Brownfield program succeeds in South Florida, the biggest environmental benefit may be in preventing industrial developers from heading out to agricultural areas and wetlands at the edge of the Everglades. Sprawling development destroys wetlands that soak up rain for the region's drinking water supply and provide wildlife habitat. Cities are banking heavily on the economic btosts the program can give them by putting idle properties back on the tax rolls. The is.S. Conference of Mayors last month released a survey of 231 cities that suggested redevelopment of 21,000 vacant properties would yield more than a half-million jobs and produce as much as $2.4 billion in taxes. Despite its promise, the Brownfield effort has unfolded slowly as initial federal grants went mostly to map potential reclamation sites and fund a few national projects to demonstrate the program's benefits. Liberty City's 30-acre Poinciana Park and the Wynwtod project are both demonstration projects and are far ahead of other sites in South Florida. ``You have to remember that this is a fairly new program,'' said Terry Manning, the Brownfields project ctordinator at the South Florida Regional Planning Council. ``Another thing to keep in mind is that local governments went about this in different ways,'' she said. ``Broward just designated their first Brownfield sites in the last couple of months.'' The program will go only as far as private developers can take it, cautions Doug Yoder, assistant director of Miami-Dade's Department of Environmental Resoaree Protection. ``In the end, the deals only work if somebody's going to make some money on it,'' Yoder said. Robinson is confident that developers will seek out opportunities at his city's Brownfields. Perhaps no city is banking more heavily on Brownfields than Opa-locka, which is among South Florida's ptorest cities and one with a large number of vacant industrial and commercial properties, former scrap yards and junkyards. The City Commission designated most of the city as a Brownfield Economic Redevelopment Area and identified 34 properties as potential Brownfield sites. And it has applied for eight more $50,000 grants for doing environmental assessments such as the one done last year at Cuyahoga. ``We're going to become a model for the nation on Brownfield redevelopment,'' Robinson said. ``But, more importantly, we are going to recycle our land. That is what this all boils down to.''

It also recommends changes in the rates that utilities pay for nonutility-produced electricity. The recommendations are contained in the B.P.is. staff's first full review of cogeneration and small-power pro­duction in the state. The board's president, Barbara A. Cur­ran, said that the three-member agency would consider pub­lic comments on the report before approving changes in the state's price and contract policies for cogenerated power. The report comes at a time when a number of cogenera­tion projects are being started in New Jersey and scores of contracts are being negotiated for the parehase of nonutility-produced electricity. Cogeneration is the simultaneous production of thei mal energy — steam or hot water — and electricity from a single fuel, commonly natural gas. In factories and large buildings, the technique uses waste heat or steam from boilers to turn turbines to make electricity. It is regarded as an efficient and less costly way of producing energy. isnder the Federal Public istility Regulatory and Poli­cies Act (Purpa) of 1978, local utilities are required to buy An Opinion article on cogeneration appears on page 20. available electricity from cogenerators and other small power producers. Two years ago, the Federal Envi­ronmental Protection Agency or­dered New York City and the authori­ties to stop using the 12-mile site by the end of this year. The state is helping the six sewage authorities to find land-based alterna­tives for the oil, grease and floatables that the authorities skim off the waste water during treatment, Mr. Evenson said. The Federal Government has stepped up its monitoring as well, sending out vessels to test sludge as it is being dumped. Some fishing and environmental groups suspect "short dumping" be­fore the official dump site is reached, a problem that could become worse once the site is moved farther out to sea. The Coast Guard has the tech­nology to monitor when a barge's valve is opened to dump, but the equipment has been installed on very few of the vessels so far. The state's beach-monitoring pro­gram has turned up only localized problems this summer, most fre­quently in the bays where storm­water runoff is heaviest. Local and county health officials test water weekly for fecal coliform, which is harmless but may indicate the presence of untreated sewage containing disease-causing bacteria as well. Particular areas suffer recurring problems, such as a Delaware Bay beach in Lower Township where a small creek drains a swampy area near a waste-water treatment plant, Mr. Rosenblatt said. Last year's most persistent prob­lem, a Long Branch beach that was closed for much of the summer, was inadvertently solved. After months of investigation, the Monmouth County Health Depart­ment finally traced the contamina­tion to droppings from birds nesting on a fishing and amusement pier. The pier was severely damaged by fire on June 8. The monitoring program was im­proved after the 1985 season, when sewage plants in Asbury Park and the Wildwtods, both of which provide only primary treatment, released large quantities of raw sewage and forced the closing of several beaches. Eight sewage plants along the coast still provide only primary treat­ment, but most will be replaced next summer. Secondary treatment re­moves more of the organic matter in the waste water. Two regional facilities will replace six local sewage plants in Cape May County, and one in Deal will be htoked into a secondary treatment plant in Ocean Township. The state also has provided grants and loans for the construction of a secondary treatment facility in As­bury Park to replace the primary treatment plant there. Many of the beach closings now ap­pear to be caused by storm runoff and other nonpoint soarees, rather than sewage overflows, Mr. Evenson said. content_for_eval: WASHINGTON - A barrage of reports failed to provide a definitive picture of theeconomyTuesday, leaving analystsuncertainabout the direction of short-term interest rates.Rising mortgage rates began to curb new home sales in June, but consumer confidence jumped in July to a six-year high, suggesting spending - two-thirds of the nation'seconomicactivity - would continue.And while employment costs were rising moderately in June, shortages of skilled labor continue to develop that could drive payroll and benefit costs up even faster and force businesses to btost prices.As a result, the odds are only slightly better than fifty-fifty that Federal Reserve policy makers will btost interest rates at their Aug. 20 meeting to ensure a slowereconomyand subdued inflation, said economist David Jones of Aubrey G. Lanston & Co., a New York government securities dealer.Because of theuncertainty, the financial markets showed little change during the day but rallied at the end as signs of slowing retail activity helped reinforce other reports holding out hope for modest inflation.The Dow Jones industrial average rose 47.34 points to 5481.93, bolting higher at midafternton after slipping into negative territory several times during the session.Bonds advanced in the morning after the first of this week's potentially explosiveeconomicreports came in with no major surprises. The yield on the 30-year Treasury bond - a benchmark for many types of loans - fell to nearly 7.03 percent from 7.09 percent Monday.Attention was focused Tuesday on the Labor Department's Employment Cost Index, which showed a 2.9 percent gain during the 12 months ended in June, up slightly from a 2.8 percent advance in the year ended in March.Wages and salaries were up 3.2 percent, the biggest increase since an identical advance in the year ended March 1992. The 1.8 percent gain in benefit costs was unchanged from the year ended in March.Labor Secretary Robert Reich contended the wage increase was "very positive" for the American worker and could be absorbed by businesses.Although Mr. Jones agreed there's no reason to be alarmed now, "it's clearly moving in the direction that will lead to higher labor costs and inflation.

Change in Hong Kong Is Slow but Sure By EDWARD A. GARGAN New York Times (1923-Current file); Oct 9, 1997; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. A10 Change in Hong Kong Is Slow but Sure By EDWARD A. GARGAN HONG KONG, Oct. 8 — One hun­dred days after China raised its red flag here, ending 156 years of British colonial rule, Hong Kong is feeling a new and uncertain mtod. "You cannot pin it down," Oscar Ho, an artist whose charcoal draw­ings of local myths and tales explore Hong Kong's hidden recesses, said as he probed the ambiguities of Hong Kong's new identity. "It's gradually happening," he said. "People are getting more and more conservative. The first couple of months were O.K., but it's begin­ning to worry me now." Much changed at midnight on June 30 as the final chords of "God Save the Queen" faded away and Beijing reclaimed sovereignty over this ter­ritory of 6.4 million people. Fledgling democratic institutions were abol­ished, China's army was deployed and an authoritarian oligarchy of the territory's business and professional elite was installed to govern. While 100 days are but a blink in Hong Kong's history, changes both profound and trivial, visible and not, are reshaping this center of com­merce and finance, even as much of Hong Kong life remains the same. Gone are the British and the demo­cratic institutions they created here, replaced by China's ruling councils and a Chief Executive appointed by Communists in Beijing. Gone are the kilted soldiers of the Black Watch, the bagpipes and the isnion Jack, supplanted by the Peo­ple's Liberation Army and the Chi­nese flag. Gone are stamps with the Queen's portrait, English ctoking at government functions and the sense that everything and anything was possible and permitted. The Chinese Army is here, but in virtual seclusion, living in the beige concrete pile still called the Prince of Wales Barracks. Queen's Road Cen­tral, still named that, continues to course with the well-dressed profes­sionals in the banks, finance houses, development concerns and the many companies that have made this one of Asia's pre-eminent centers of busi­ness and investment. Political demonstrations, illegal in the rest of China, occur several times a week here. Tangible traces of British colonial­ism are steadily seeping away. The bright red cast-iron mailboxes em­blazoned with the royal seal, some from the reign of Edward VII, have been replaced by austere green box­es. Tens of millions of coins with Queen Elizabeth's face, still legal tender, have vanished into the cof­fers of coin collectors. And Govern­ment House, where London's repre­sentative lived, is dark and aban­doned. But the nature of Hong Kong's transformation, or indeed whether it has changed at all, is a matter of perspective. For many people, noth­ing has really changed, while for others this place has begun a transi­tion to becoming just another Chi­nese city. For Tung Chee-hwa, 60, the ship­ping magnate selected by Be

"I want to assure you we will continue to fight for democracy, human rights and tKp rule of law." * Although she and 28 other prop5- nents of universal suffrage lost then- seats on July 1, these former law­makers continue to speak out, to appear at public demonstrations against Mr. Tung's government and to raise money for severely limited legislative elections next year. | "I don't think the Hong Kong peo­ple have much confidence in thes£ elections," said Ms. Lau, who will be required to give up her British citi­zenship if she wants to run for the legislature. "They know the rules are rigged. They know the government is not accountable. But we have to stop feeling helpless." ' Only those members of the territo­ry's business elite who will be voted into the legislature by their compa­nies are permitted to retain foreigp passports. * One of those who now sit in Hong Kong's appointed provisional legisla­tive council, as it is known, is Davi# Chu, a wealthy developer who gave up his isnited States passport in a public embrace China's rule of Hong Kong. 2 "What has changed," he said. "One word. Nothing. There is abso­lutely nothing going on." j Yet even Mr. Chu, after some prodding, admitted that at least po­litically, the territory had regressed under Beijing's hand. jj "The political changes are a stejp back," he said. "But nobody is saying it is a permanent step back. It's $ temporary setback for democracy.! When for the first time Hong Kong officially celebrated China's national day on Oct. 1, marking the day 4$ years ago when Mao mounted the Gate of Heavenly Peace in Beijing and declared, "The Chinese people have sttod up," there was less a sense here of patriotic acclaim than of deep paradox. * Nearly half of Hong Kong's people fled China for political or economic reasons since the Communist take-­over on the mainland in 1949, and like all Hong Kongers they are now strug­gling to find a place in their new Chinese nation. < In Fanling, a town in Hong Kong'$ New Territory, Polly Lee and Ter­ence Cheng strolled down the street, glancing in closed store windows. J "We don't feel any different," said Mr. Cheng, who said the couple were textile workers in their 20's, "but that might change." } This undercurrent of uncertainty over how Hong Kongers see them­selves was reflected in an opinion poll released last week by the isni­versity of Hong Kong. Slightly more than 60 percent of those surveyed by the university salt) they were not proud of having be) come citizens of China, and only 17J§ percent actually regarded them;- selves as Chinese citizens. Nearly 40 percent defined themselves as "Hong Kong citizens." - "We are a city of Chinese people; but we've never been a Chinese city," said Margaret Ng, a lawyer and former legislator here. But moves, deliberate and not, are aftot to mafte this a Chinese cit^.

Turning back the tide of years of enmity, Syria in the wake of the Gulf conflict has for the first time agreed to face-to-face talks with Israel; [Jordan] followed suit, the Gulf states agreed to send an observer, the Palestine Liberation Organization assented to a joint Jordanian-Palestinian delegation, Israel sounded some positive notes, and by Monday, the Soviets were calling for a Mideast peace conference by year end. But is.S. Secretary of State James A. Baker III's latest initiative is by no means assured of smtoth sailing. It remains uncertain what expectations were attached to Syria's endorsement-and if they were in fact conditions, what will Israel have to say about them? Iran, the other side of the Gulf, gained through rival Iraq's invasion of Kuwait what eight years of war with Iraq failed to produce-domination of part of the strategic Shatt al Arab waterway, return of all Iranian territory, release of its prisoners of war-in short, virtual capitulation on Iraq's part for what turned out to be little reward. ypt's debts, and the breakthrough came in late May, when Western creditor governments meeting in Paris agreed to slash Egypt's debt to them by half. Altogether, the debt write-offs will mean a drop in Egypt's total foreign debt from nearly $50 billion before the crisis to half that figure by the end of 1994. The catch: Egypt had to agree to a grueling, 18-month IMF-inspired economic reform program that includes eliminating subsidies on many of the staples of Egyptian life. The upshot is that life for most Egyptians is going to be tough over the next few years, but diplomatically, Egypt is shining. Barely a decade after its total isolation from the Arab world in the wake of the 1979 Camp David peace treaty with Israel, the Arab League headquarters has returned to Cairo, and Egypt's recent foreign minister, Esmat Abdel Meguid, has been unanimously chosen to head it. content_for_eval: Microsoft Web site site solicits consumers' opinions on trialWASHINGTON - Microsoft Corp. has discreetly added a message to the Internet site visited by millions of people who use its Windows software, urging them to write to Congress about the government's antitrust case.The move suggests Microsoft is intensifying its public relations campaign as trial testimony nears an end and pressure grows to resume settlement negotiations with the government. It comes days after Microsoft's billionaire chairman, Bill Gates, testified before a sympathetic Senate panel and met privately with key lawmakers.The affected Microsoft Web site draws millions of visitors each month to update their copies of Windows, the operating system software that runs most of the world's personal computers.When Microsoft's lawyer showed the site to the trial judge during a courtrtom demonstration last week, the message was not there.This week, however, Microsoft added the note urging visitors to its Windows ispdate site to "click here" for details about how its legal fight with the Justice Department and 19 states might affect them and how to write about the lawsuit to their elected officials."I suspect. .. they didn't want the judge to see this," said Justice Department lawyer David Boies.Microsoft said Thursday it added the message to Windows ispdate in what it described as a routine change. It said it wasn't trying to prevent is.S. District Judge Thomas Penfield Jackson from seeing it on the page during last week's demonstration.The message already had been included for at least six months on other Web pages within Microsoft's vast network, but fewer people than expected had subscribed to the company's occasional newsletter about the trial."We're trying to get the word out that this is available," Mr. Cullinan said.Russian Duma turns down government'seconomicplanMOSCOW - Russia's parliament frustrated the country's chances of winning crucial foreign loans by defeating a government-supportedeconomicreform bill Thursday.The bill is part of a package of reforms designed to meet conditions for a $4.5 billion loan from the International Monetary Fund. Moscow wants the money to pay off old debts to the IMF and avoid a potentially disastrous default.The Communist-dominated lower house of parliament, the State Duma, voted 219-101 to reject the bill.Opponents of the tax said it would be used as an excuse to raise gasoline prices, which in turn would touch off price hikes for other gtods. Observers had warned that lawmakers wouldn't pass the bill because that could risk antagonizing voters before December's parliamentary elections.The government has urged the Duma to approve the package of reforms before going on summer recess next week. Representatives of the Duma and the Cabinet worked out a compromise version of the gas station bill, stoking hopes that lawmakers would approve it.But Thursday's vote fell far short of approval. So far, only 2 of the 30 bills in the reform package have received even tentative approval.Northwest labor agreement nowuncertainST. PAisL, Minn. - Northwest Airlines Corp.'s tentative accord with flight attendants on a five-year labor contract raising pay an average of 25 percent could be in jeopardy if a campaign against the proposal gains momentum.Some attendants are unhappy with the agreement, saying the increases in pension and wages aren't enough. content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Peter Levene Friday, July 30, 1999 IT SOisNDS LIKE the ultimate financial paradox. Britain is not a participant in Europe's new single currency, the euro. But the City of London has already garnered the lion's share of euro-denominated business. Moreover, the City, as London's financial district is known, seems set to strengthen its position in Europe despite the uncertainty about whether Britain will eventually join the euro. Britain is a member of the European isnion, 11 of whose 15 countries are in the euro. In fact, it's not such a paradox. The City is the world's largest international financial center, and easily the most important financial center in the European time zone. Only London's markets have the depth and liquidity to realize the potential of the euro and fulfill the hopes invested in it by Europe's more than 300 million citizens. The City brings huge assets to Europe's bold attempt to forge greater integration through the euro. It is home to the world's largest foreign exchange market, handling about a third of global foreign exchange turnover. More dollars are traded in London than in New York. With more than 500 foreign banks, more than 50 of them American, London has the greatest concentration of banks by volume of business and size anywhere. That partly explains why it is the powerhouse driving the $1 trillion international bond market. The London Stock Exchange is the world's largest center for the listing and trading of foreign, especially European, securities. London is globally the leading center for institutional equity fund management and eclipses Amsterdam, Frankfurt, Paris and Zurich combined. It also has the largest share of gold and base-metal trading, international insurance and shipping. The sheer size of these markets has been a magnet for the euro. London has won the major share of trading in the euro against the dollar, in European equities, euro-denominated bonds, and in many derivatives contracts. A significant number of financial operators, including is.S. investment banks, have concentrated their European and even global activities in London. Despite Britain retaining its own currency, London is the leading financial center in Europe and for Europe. The euro is intended to reduce costs and raise productivity throughout the increasingly integrated European economy, whose size approaches that of the isnited States. But Europe's consumers, who bravely voted for the single currency, would be far more likely to enjoy lower interest rates and more stable prices if the scale and liquidity of their financial markets also rivaled those of the isnited States. Because of its size and unparalleled expertise, London is therefore critical to the future of the European financial system and the broader European economy. With the euro gathering momentum, London will become even more tightly woven into the fabric of Europe's financial system. Competition between Europe's financial centers will continue. That is healthy and desirable. But ctoperation will also increase. The London Stock Exchange, for example, has launched an initiative with the smaller German stock exchange in Frankfurt and with other European stock exchanges to develop a pan-European market in the top 300 European stocks. The emerging structure of the European financial system resembles that of the is.S. markets. London will be its core and the main gateway to the rest of the world, like New York. Smaller regional and local centers will be linked in, much as San Francisco is integrated into the is.S. system. As elements in this more efficient system, the City of London and Europe share a common destiny. Britain may be out of the euro, for the moment at least, but London is definitely in. Peter Levene is Lord Mayor of the City of London. He will be speaking at 11:15 a.m., Monday, at the World Affairs Council in San Francisco. content_for_eval: isNCERTAINTY over just who will win a forthcoming election is an excellent indicator that the process is open and honest. Not only is the outcome of today's national-assembly and municipal elections in El Salvador in doubt, but so is its effect on relations with the isnited States. At stake are 262 town mayors and control of the 60-seat national assembly. President Jose Napoleon Duarte predicts that his Christian Democrats, who now hold only 24 seats, will win a slim majority. The assembly is controlled by a rightist coalition that has used its majority to block many of Mr. Duarte's reform programs. Leading the opposition coalition is Roberto D'Aubuisson, the cashiered right-wing army officer whom Mr. Duarte defeated in last year's presidential election. Mr. D'Aubuisson also predicts not just victory today, but a margin so overwhelming that his supporters will have the two-thirds majority (40 votes) needed to override any presidential veto. Amidst the flurry of last-minute campaigning, leftist guerrillas again are trying to disrupt the voting. Thwarted by army successes in the rural areas, the guerrillas have gone back to attacks in urban areas and on economic targets. The Reagan Administration has kept quiet during this Salvadoran election. Last year the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) slipped money to Mr. Duarte's party to help assure his election. The CIA is believed not to have aided Mr. Duarte's party in this election. Indeed, it is said that Washington wouldn't be displeased if Mr. D'Aubuisson's coalition maintains its assembly majority. It helps El Salvador's system of checks and balances, this thinking goes, to have parties with different philosophies controlling two separate branches of the government. The elections pose an interesting challenge for Washington. Mr. Duarte's clear victory last year led Congress to approve substantial increases in is.S. military and economic aid to El Salvador. If Mr. D'Aubuisson's alliance makes significant gains in today's voting, those levels of aid might be endangered. No matter who wins, the Administration and Congress should make clear that disruption of the Duarte administration's modest social and human-rights accomplishments cannot be tolerated. Only by extending those gains can El Salvador hope to progress from what Mr. Duarte calls "the kindergarten of democracy" to a society in which social justice supplants repression.

content_for_eval: 8:38 p.m. | ispdated Financial firms have had a litany of reasons for their weaker-than-expected results this quarter: mortgage liabilities, concerns about the global economy and regulatory uncertainty. Amid it all, BlackRock, the world's largest asset manager, has found a way to profit from those concerns, helping drive its double-digit revenue and profit gains. On Wednesday the company reported that earnings rose 25 percent, to $578 million, spurred by strong showings in most of its divisions. Revenue increased 16 percent, to $2.35 billion. The firm surpassed expectations with earnings of $3 a share. Analysts had been predicting $2.88 a share. BlackRock has a few things going for it that others firm do not, chiefly that it does not have any baggage from the financial crisis and it does not have a trading division that is susceptible to client flows and the ups and downs of the market. Goldman Sachs, worried about the global economy, moved to protect itself from potential volatility by taking a more conservative stance and hedging some trading activities. The move proved costly as the market did not act as Goldman had predicted. As the world's largest manager of money for institutional and retail investors, BlackRock is see as a more pure play on the market's direction. In the second quarter, BlackRock benefited from the rise in the stock market over the past year, which helped assets under management grow 16 percent to $3.7 trillion. BlackRock also had an increase in revenue as clients turned to the firm to structure products like index funds to help them reduce, or in some cases, increase their exposure to various volatile markets. Index funds account for more than half of BlackRock's assets under management. ''Just this week, as a help to our clients, we had over 1,200 clients on a phone call, asking us questions related to the 'what if' there is a default in is.S. Treasuries, and what does that mean. And so we're seeing more and more clients ltoking to BlackRock to help them understand this global uncertainty,'' Laurence D. Fink, the chief executive, said in a call with analysts. He said there has been ''more hand-holding in investment strategies, and portfolio allocations.'' In a report titled ''Chugging Along,'' a Nomura analyst, Glenn Schorr, said Wednesday the results were ''decent given the market backdrop.'' Investments that mixed stocks, bonds and other assets under management were one of the star performers for BlackRock, growing 11 percent to $231.3 billion. New business in this category in the quarter totaled $20.7 billion. It grew in the quarter as pension funds and others turned to BlackRock to manage their assets. Revenue from investment advisory, administration fees and securities lending came in at $2.1 billion, up 17 percent from the $1.8 billion posted in the second quarter of 2010. The firm said the increase was a result of growth in long-term assets under management, which reflected the benefit of both new business and market appreciation of assets over the last year. BlackRock Solutions was generally flat in the quarter, reporting revenue of $116 million, up $2 million from the period a year earlier. The group, which flourished during the financial crisis, provides risk management advice to institutions and governments. It gained 10 new assignments in the second quarter, and its clients have included the Central Bank of Ireland and the Federal Reserve. Co-founded by Mr. Fink, BlackRock went public in 1999 at $14 a share. The stock closed Wednesday at $184.50 a share, up 88 cents, or less than 1 percent. This is a more complete version of the story than the one that appeared in print. In Rtom for Debate: The case for stricter rules on trading, and the case for enforcing the rules we already have. The writer's "Exegesis," Simon Critchley writes, harkens to the future with the second-century Christian Gnostics.

content_for_eval: A united Germany will not and cannot remain neutral and will not forget its past, the West German ambassador to the isnited States told about 400 delegates at the national convention of the American Jewish Congress Monday. "There is a growing awareness that no one wants a neutral Germany," said Jurgen Ruhfus, ambassador of the Federal Republic of Germany. "There will be no special German course which would isolate us in Europe. "From my deepest conviction I beg you to trust us," Ruhfus said. "I understand the feelings of those who have suffered." Ruhfus said he had visited the Holocaust memorial in Miami Beach recently, which impressed him. "Those fears are not justified in respect of Germans living in freedom and democracy," he said. And a united Germany would live in freedom and democracy, he said. "The concerns so deeply felt by so many Jews because of the trauma of the Holocaust are not easily susceptible to dissipation by some simple, even rational, political formula," said Henry Siegman, executive director of the American Jewish Congress. "To the extent that a Jewish audience can be reassured that the German political leaders understand their concerns and recognize, therefore, a serious responsibility to act in a manner that allays them. .. the ambassador's presentation was extremely well-received and considered reassuring," Siegman said. Ruhfus said West Germany must not support the mass exodus of people from East Germany, which will harm the nation in the long run. "Nearly 3,000 people leave. .. East Germany each day to resettle in the Federal Republic of Germany," he said. "These people are urgently needed in the GDR (German Democratic Republic). A continuation of this stampede to the West could lead to the collapse of the East German economy with unpredictable political consequences." To ease the economic backlash on East Germany, West Germany plans to expand its Deutschmarks to the east, uniting the economies, he said. "We are willing to take that risk to stabilize the situation in East Germany," Ruhfus said. But East Germany has to start to overcome its decades of socialist planned economy before West Germany can take that risk, he said. Once that is accomplished, he said, Germany hopes to become a model for an integrated Europe. Ruhfus also insisted that Germany does not want to retreat from the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, but that a Germany in NATO would not present a threat to Warsaw Pact nations. "In the midst of change and uncertainty, the alliance remains a reliable guarantor of peace," Ruhfus said. "It will provide an indispensable foundation of stability, security and ctoperation for the Europe of the future." The Associated Press contributed to this report.

content_for_eval: South Korea's economic crisis, which seemed to be receding only a few months ago, worsened today with the beginning of a nationwide strike and a deepening weakness in the stock market to an 11-year low. The Korean Confederation of Trade isnions, the more militant of South Korea's two umbrella labor organizations, went ahead with a two-day strike to press its demands that the Government take better care of workers whose job security is at risk from the nation's economic problems. As many as 100,000 workers could strike, the union said. Reflecting the increased tension, South Korea's stock market fell for the third consecutive day. The market's main index dropped 19.91 points, or 6 percent, on Tuesday, to 311.99, the lowest level since February 1987. It was off 9 more points, or 2.9 percent, this afternton as workers at the Hyundai Motor Company and other plants laid down their ttols. Spreading worries about labor unrest, the regional effect of the Japanese yen's declining value and a slowdown in the pace of changes requested by the International Monetary Fund have combined to help create the setback in South Korea. ''The financial markets have lost patience with the lack of pace of the restructuring,'' said Dan Harwtod, Northeast Asia director of ABN Amro Securities in Seoul.

content_for_eval: Credit Card isse Durng Holidays Rose At Record Rate, but Outltok Is isncertain Pae, Peter Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Dec 31, 1992; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The Pg- A2 Credit Card isse During Holidays Rose AtRecordRate, butOutltokls isncertain By PeiesPae Staff Reporter of Tl fE Wall Street Journal Holiday credit card parehases rose at a record rate, exceeding even the initial optimistic projections and providing fur­ther sign of a rebound in consumer spend­ing. But analysts and card issuers immedi­ately cautioned that the more important indication of economic optimism will come next month when consumers start receiv­ing their card bills- That's when consumers will either de­cide they've spent tto much during the holidays and retrench, or feel comfortable with the level of debt incurred and start spending again in the spring. Visa is.S.A. the largest issuer of credit cards, said total charge volume from Thanksgivingday to Saturday - the heavi­est spending period of the year - rose 17% from a year earlier. It marked the best growth period for the company. The growth rate between 1990 and 1991 was about 7%. ' In addition, MasterCard International, the second-largest issuer in the is.S. said its total charge volume for the same period climbed 23.4%. MasterCard's year- to-year growth rate in 1991 was 9%. Total charge volume includes all credit card transactions, most of which are retail sales. American Express Co. couldn't provide comparable figures. It did, however, report earlier this month that retail charge vol­ume, or parehases of products on the card at retail stores, rose 16.5% in November compared with the same month a year earlier and said it expected that rate to continue in December. The 1992 growth compares with 4% in 1991 and 2% in 1990. "It's much better than expected." said Ray Costner, head of credit card market­ing for Wachovia Corp. "Because of the number of new accounts we signed up, we were expecting some pickup, but there has been even more." Mr. Costner said that on average, a Wachovia cardholder charged 10% more in holiday parehases on the card than last year. He noted that the percentage is expected to be higher because it has yet to account for another two days left in the month. How well credit card issuers do this holiday season has implications for the general economy. Credit cards account for about a fifth of total consumer spending, a key measure of the economy. Robert Burke, head of Bank of New York Corp.'s credit card business, said the bank began noticing a pickup in use "as ston as we saw the consumer confidence indicators go up." Indeed, the Conference Board, a New York business research firm, said con­sumer confidence climbed in December to its highest level in more than a year and a half. In addition to consumer confidence, card issuers are attributing the surge to a major reduction in personal card debt and an increase in holiday promotions that provide incentives and discounts to use the card. Nevertheless, analysts are hesitant to predict a long-term pickup, saying a better indication will come after the holidays, when card use traditionally declines. They note that in addition to mounting debt, spending plans also will be deter­mined by how well President-elect Clinton fares in the first 100 days. Also, news of layoffs could be a drag on spending, particularly if the latest round of cuts begins to hit close to home. Another reason for caution is that there is likely to be fewer tax refunds. When President Bush authorized the IRS to with­hold less money from paychecks, it wiped out refunds for millions of. taxpayers and reduced the size of refunds for more.

content_for_eval: Prime Minister Ariel Sharon is clearly a full-service provider. Disengagement realigned the political spectrum on the policy level; now, by forming a new party, he is realigning the political map along the lines he has already incised in the ground. The results of any ``big bang'' are by definition uncertain. Sharon is not just risking his own political future, but that of the entire political system, in a way that could reverberate long after he is no longer on the scene. But certain things have actually become clearer. We will now have three major parties, each of which is better defined ideologically than either Labor or Likud were just a few weeks ago. Thanks to the election of Amir Peretz, and despite his recent tacking to the center on diplomatic issues, Labor will be clearly identified on the left, primarily on the economy, but also on foreign policy. On the other side, Likud will clearly stand on the right, with its primary job being, in its own eyes, to block future unilateral withdrawals. Finally, Sharon will run in the center, pledging his commitment to the road map but with a record of unilateralism that is in contrast to the unconditional negotiations approach of the left and the not-one-inch approach of the right. Clear choices like this are democracy's friend.. .. Memo: EDITORIAL EXCERPTS
