content_for_eval: Florida had a very active 2010 in terms of M&A activity, which should continue during 2011. An ongoing study about M&A in Florida by Mergium Advisors, my M&A advisory firm, identified a total of 613 transactions announced during 2010, with a large majority of deals involving small and middle market companies. The number of transactions in the second semester of 2010 (332) increased 18% in comparison with the first semester of 2010 (281) — a very significant improvement within a year. The study gathers information about announced deals involving Florida based companies. Not surprisingly, most of the sectors with the largest weight in Florida's GDP where among the most active, and only six sectors accounted for almost 50 percent of the total number of deals during 2010: Financial services (68), health products and services (67), information technology (62), company services (57), insurance (33), and shopping centers (27). Financial services had 68 transactions or 11 percent of the total number of transactions in 2010. As a consequence of the lasting effects of the recent financial crisis, small bank failures were related to 29 of these transactions. This was a large increase when compared to 14 bank failures in 2009. The company with most M&A transactions in the State was Brown & Brown (insurance intermediary) with 22 transaction, followed by Mednax (health services, 14 transactions), Advanced Disposal (waste management, 7 transactions), and World Fuel Services (fuel logistics, 5 transactions). Private equity firms were involved in 17 percent of the transactions. The largest private equity firms based in the state were very active. Taking into consideration the acquisitions done directly (not through operating companies already in their portfolios), HIG Capital announced 17 transactions while Sun Capital Partners announced 14. Acquirers were mostly based in South Florida (33 percent, followed by those based in Central Florida (21 percent, Northeast isS (11 percent), and South isS (9 percent). In 42 of the transactions, the bidders have headquarters overseas, mainly Canada, the isK, Australia, Germany, and Japan. Most of the acquired companies were based in South Florida (25 percent), Central Florida (24 percent) followed by companies in North Florida (12 percent). In 40 transactions, the targets had headquarters overseas, including the isK, Canada, Germany, the Netherlands, Brazil, Denmark, France, Mexico, and Spain. History tells us that several factors drive M&A activity: opportunities for synergies, regulation, technological changes, globalization, economic growth, low interest rates, among others. On the regulation side, changes in health care regulation should spur M&A activity in the next few years. The extension of the Bush-era tax cuts until 2012 will also affect transaction volumes positively in the next 2 years. Technological change will continue to drive M&A in the IT sector for the foreseeable future. In financial services, the trend will depend on bank failures this year. There will be additional bank failures in Florida. The question is how many banks will fail. Like the is.S. as a whole, Florida's economic output has been increasing since mid-2009. Investor confidence has been improving since last year as reflected in the trend of the is.S. stock indexes and other confidence indicators. Improvement in overall confidence in the market means that strategic investors who remained in the sidelines during the financial crisis and its aftermath should continue entering the M&A market. Non-financial companies are showing cash-rich balance sheets. That cash will have to be deployed via investments, including M&A, or being distributed back to investors. Sustained low financial costs have helped M&A activity. Low interest rates in the next few months should be a positive element. Global uncertainties remain an issue. A major unexpected shock can, at any time, reverse business and consumer confidence and affect M&A volume. Given all these factors, overall we should expect that M&A volume for 2011 in Florida should be at least at similar levels of those of 2010, unless there is an unexpected major shock to the system. Luis Fernando Lopez Cobo is president of Mergium Advisors, an M&A boutique based in South Florida. He is also adjunct professor at the isniversity of Miami.

NTT's Cutbacks Signal isncertain Transition in Japan: Economy and ... Schlesinger, Jacob;Hamilton, David Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Feb 26, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The pg. B4 — CORPORATE FOCisS By Jacob M. Schlesinger And David P. Hamilton Staff Reporters of Ton Wall Stiif.kt Journal TOKYO - When Nippon Telegraph & Telephone Corp. announces today that It will slash staff and close offices, It will spotlight a major economic struggle under­way In Japan: the bumpy transition from cozy, controlled stability to the more uncertain free market. Japan's huge part-public, part-private telecommunications centaur - under at­tack by new competitors but tightly reined in by regulators, publicly traded but ma­jority-owned by the government - will un­veil a new cost-cutting plan likely to trim its payroll by more than 10%, or 30,000 employees, as well as a consolidation of many offices and retail outlets over the next three years, "The market struggle Is getting harder and harder," says an NTT spokesman. "We must become more efficient." Critics say NTT's new plan will barely scratch the surface. "In the long run, they will have to cut more," says Sachio Sem- moto, who quit NTT about a decade ago out of frustration with the bureaucratic com­pany and who now helps run one of its biggest competitors, DDI Corp. "Other­wise they will not survive," The catalyst for NTT's actions is tum­bling profits. The company estimates its pretax profit in the year ending March 31 will drop 35% from a year ago to 229 billion yen ($1.95 billion). NTT says that number could fall by another one-third from this year's depressed level. NTT, which had revenue last fiscal year of more than six trillion yen, is so big that its tremors are likely to hurt companies throughout this country. The company is Japan's largest employer, its biggest capital spender, and accounts for an estimated 3% of the Tokyo stock market's total capitalization. Clearly, the lackluster state of Japan's economy is one big cause of NTT's pain. But at the heart of the company's troubles is a simple fact: It was never designed to run efficiently, by free-market standards. In addition to providing basic phone serv­ices, the company has long been Japan Inc.'s sugar daddy, doling out technologi­cal gtodies and big contracts to other corporations, lavish contributions to politi­cians, and high-paying jobs to workers in the telecommunications union. in a deregulated and increasingly in­ternational market, NTT is finding it more difficult to balance those missions with the bottom line. In addition to cutting em­ployees, "now NTT Is putting severe requirements on its suppliers, telling us we have to cut prices because NTT's profits are bad," complains an executive at one supplier, NTT played a big role nurturing its main suppliers - NEC Corp., Fujitsu Ltd., Hitachi Ltd., and Okl Electric Indus­try Co.-by giving them a steady stream of contract business and paying inflated prices for it. In fac

[...TRUNCATED...]

on of small regional companies later grabbed nearly 40% of the market. Regulators: Just Crying Wolf NTT complains that government regu­lators are limiting its ability to fight back. The telecommunications ministry won't allow NTT to raise new revenue by in­creasing its local phone rates-a remarka­bly cheap 10 yen (8.5 cents) for three minutes-or to compete more effectively in long-distance by cutting rates as much as it wants. But the regulators seem to think NTT is crying wolf. Some dismiss the company's dire statements as grandstanding, and say the personnel cuts are just clever publicrelations moves meant to convince the public that NTT deserves its rate rise. Today's restructuring Is also part of a bigger battle over NTT's ultimate fat<v since the ministry still wants to trim the fat and break up the company in 1995, mirror­ing the breakup of American Telephone & Telegraph Co. In the is.S. As it is, the company pays salaries closer to Japan's top-notch banks than to manufacturers. Rather than easing out workers whose jobs became obsolete be­cause of automation, NTT opened a slew of unnecessary sales offices and staffed them with the redundant employees. Even the latest round of cuts will likely be handled as gently as possible. Wiitfe there may be an early retirement plan, the NTT spokesman says there won't be any layoffs. As an NTT planning manager in the early 1980s, DDI's Mr. Semmoto says he once floated the idea of cutting the com­pany's work force down to 160,000 em­ployees. "Of course, they cannot cut, so they transfer their employees to their subsidiaries," he says, noting that the subsidiaries bear the added costs by charg­ing more to their NTT contracts. Many of the employees affected by the latest reduc­tions will also likely end up at NTT affili­ates. • As if NTT management's job wasn't tough enough, there's one other group to placate: the long-suffering shareholders. When the company went public in 1987, Japanese citizens eagerly bought the stock, and the share price soared in the. first few months. Many critics later ar­gued, however, that the initial public offer:.. ing was rigged. The powerful finance,, ministry, they say, convinced securities;- houses to hype the stock: The brokerages); got their high fixed commissions, and the" government raked in the proceeds from the- mammoth sale. NTT's shares couldn't defy gravity for'- long, however, and crashed even before';; the broader market did. The stock is now'1 trading at 80% below its peak. Thosev shareholders who invested their life sav-'u ings In NTT shares may get some satisfac-;; tion from the fact that they're not alone:-;- The government, which arguably created;; the mess in the first place, now needs to.'i raise money, but also is loathe to dump its. content_for_eval: With mounds of lead waste smoldering at an abandoned battery-recycling plant outside town, toxic garbage piling up at the local dumps and industrial discharges flowing untreated into rivers and sewers, a new environmental enforcement agency opened offices here last fall promising to crack down as never before. Ramiro Zaragoza, the young lawyer in charge, hired 33 inspectors, outfitted them in green windbreakers and began training them to take on the malfeasants. In Mexico City, officials announced a new dawn in the country's fight against pollution, new prtof that if Mexico was allowed to join Canada and the isnited States in the North American Free Trade Agreement it would not sacrifice its environment to attract business across the border. Toxic Legacy of Decades But in Tijuana it was not that easy. Other agencies would not lend their files on the city's 700 industrial plants, officials in and outside the enforcement office say. Laboratory studies could not be done for lack of equipment or supplies. Then, for five months and without explanation, paychecks did not arrive. Mr. Zaragoza says he is as determined as ever, but only five of his inspectors are still on the job.
